findings.title: Unrestricted Oracle Price Setting
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle contract allows anyone to set the price for any token without any access control. This enables arbitrary manipulation of the token price at any time.
findings.message: An attacker can manipulate the token price to liquidate other users' positions at a discount, or to self-liquidate to avoid debt, or to inflate collateral to borrow more than allowed, effectively draining the vault of funds.
findings.recommendation: Implement access control on setPrice (e.g., only owner) and use a TWAP oracle resistant to manipulation. Consider using a trusted price feed like Chainlink.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Liquidation Does Not Update totalBorrowed and Does Not Require Debt Repayment
findings.description: The liquidate function sets the user's borrowed balance to zero without updating the global totalBorrowed variable. Additionally, it does not require the liquidator to repay the outstanding debt; it simply transfers the collateral to the liquidator. This leads to accounting inconsistency and allows an attacker to self-liquidate by manipulating oracle price, effectively stealing the borrowed amount.
findings.message: An attacker can borrow tokens, manipulate oracle to crash price, self-liquidate, and walk away with the borrowed tokens without ever repaying, causing direct loss of funds from the vault. The accounting error also prevents other users from withdrawing correctly.
findings.recommendation: Update totalBorrowed in liquidate: totalBorrowed -= borrowed[user]; Also require the liquidator to repay the debt or transfer collateral to the vault for proper debt coverage.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deposits 100 tokens as collateral. 2. Attacker borrows 60 tokens. 3. Attacker calls oracle.setPrice(token, 1) to reduce collateral value to near zero. 4. Attacker calls vault.liquidate(attacker). Condition passes, collateral[attacker]=0, borrowed[attacker]=0, and 100 tokens are transferred to attacker. totalBorrowed remains 60. Attacker now has both the borrowed 60 and the returned 100 (net gain 60). Vault is insolvent.

findings.title: Liquidation does not require debt repayment, causing vault insolvency
findings.description: The `liquidate` function transfers the entire collateral of the liquidated user to the liquidator without requiring the liquidator to repay the user's debt. The debt mapping `borrowed[user]` is set to zero, but the vault receives no tokens in return, causing a net asset loss. The missing update to `totalBorrowed` further aggravates the state inconsistency. This allows any user with a friend to artificially drain the vault: user deposits collateral, borrows, then a friend liquidates the position (even if not undercollateralized by manipulating the oracle) and receives the collateral for free, while the vault loses both the collateral and the debt repayment.
findings.message: Complete loss of protocol solvency; all user deposits and collateral can be extracted since a borrower can be liquidated at will (or by colluding with someone) and the vault's assets decrease without receiving repayment.
findings.recommendation: Redesign liquidation to require the liquidator to repay the user's debt (or seize only the minimum collateral needed). Update `totalBorrowed` by subtracting the liquidated debt. Consider using a partial liquidation mechanism.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Missing Reentrancy Protection on State-Changing Functions During Flash Loan
findings.description: The flashLoan function uses a boolean lock to prevent reentrant calls to itself, but it does not protect other state-changing functions (borrow, repay, liquidate, withdraw, deposit, addCollateral, removeCollateral). An attacker can call these functions during the callback, potentially exploiting the temporarily borrowed tokens or manipulated oracle price.
findings.message: During a flash loan, the attacker can call borrow, withdraw, or liquidate while holding borrowed tokens, enabling double-spending or accounting manipulation. Combined with oracle manipulation, this allows more sophisticated attacks.
findings.recommendation: Use a reentrancy guard modifier (e.g., OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard) on all state-changing functions, or carefully track a state variable to prevent reentrancy entirely.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: Borrow Function Relies on Manipulable Spot Price
findings.description: The borrow function calculates the maximum borrow amount based on current collateral value using getTokenPrice(), which returns a spot price from the oracle. Since the oracle price can be arbitrarily set, an attacker can temporarily inflate the price to borrow far more than their collateral is worth.
findings.message: An attacker can drain the vault by borrowing more than legitimate collateral allows, using a flash loan to manipulate the price and repay later.
findings.recommendation: Use a time-weighted average price (TWAP) oracle and require price from a trusted source. Also, implement a sanity check on price changes.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Missing update of `totalBorrowed` in `liquidate`
findings.description: When `liquidate` sets `borrowed[user] = 0`, it does not decrease `totalBorrowed`. This breaks the invariant that `totalBorrowed` equals the sum of all individual `borrowed` balances. Over time, `totalBorrowed` becomes overstated, which can cause the `borrow` function's liquidity check (`totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount`) to revert incorrectly (due to underflow in Solidity 0.8), leading to a denial of service for legitimate borrowing. Additionally, the overstated `totalBorrowed` misrepresents the vault's liabilities.
findings.message: Permanent denial of service for borrowing if many liquidations occur; incorrect accounting of protocol liabilities.
findings.recommendation: Add `totalBorrowed -= borrowed[user];` inside `liquidate` after setting `borrowed[user] = 0`.

findings.title: `removeCollateral` can bypass collateralization check via oracle manipulation
findings.description: The `removeCollateral` function uses `getTokenPrice()` to compute the new collateral value. An attacker can manipulate the oracle (e.g., through the `ManipulableOracle.setPrice` which is public) to temporarily set a high price, then call `removeCollateral` to withdraw an amount that would otherwise make the position undercollateralized. After removing the collateral, the price is restored. This effectively allows a borrower to remove collateral without being flagged as undercollateralized, increasing the protocol's risk.
findings.message: Borrowers can extract excess collateral, leaving positions undercollateralized and increasing the likelihood of bad debt.
findings.recommendation: Use a time-weighted average price (TWAP) or a trusted oracle that cannot be manipulated in a single transaction. Additionally, enforce a maximum per-block change in the oracle price or use a two-step withdrawal with a cooldown.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Flash loan callback can manipulate vault state and economic invariants

location.function: `flashLoan` function and the interplay with other functions (lines 50-80)

findings.description: During a flash loan callback, the receiver can call any of the vault's external functions (`deposit`, `withdraw`, `borrow`, `repay`, `addCollateral`, `removeCollateral`) and also manipulate the oracle price via the public `setPrice`. This allows an attacker to temporarily inflate deposits, borrow against inflated collateral, or liquidate positions at manipulated prices. The only post-flash check is the vault token balance, which can be satisfied by re-depositing borrowed funds or using liquidated collateral. This enables complex attacks that break the invariant that collateral and debt must remain consistent over a single transaction.
findings.message: Potential to extract value from the protocol, create bad debt, or cause user losses through manipulation of prices and state during a single transaction.
findings.recommendation: Implement a proper reentrancy guard that blocks all state-changing functions during a flash loan. Alternatively, isolate the flash loan function's state from other operations (e.g., use a separate contract for flash loans). Additionally, prevent oracle price changes during flash loans by using a snapshot mechanism.

findings.title: Flash loan does not enforce repayment from the receiver, relying solely on vault balance
findings.description: The `flashLoan` function expects the vault token balance after the callback to be at least `balanceBefore + fee`. However, this check can be bypassed if the attacker manipulates the vault balance during the callback via deposits/withdrawals or liquidations. For example, the attacker could deposit the borrowed amount back into the vault, artificially increasing the balance, then withdraw it after the flash loan completes (via a separate transaction). While the lock prevents reentrancy into `flashLoan`, the attacker can still use other functions to artificially inflate the balance. This undermines the core security assumption of flash loans.
findings.message: Flash loans may not be properly repaid, allowing attackers to borrow tokens without returning them, leading to loss of funds for other depositors.
findings.recommendation: Instead of a balance check, enforce repayment by transferring the principal and fee back from the receiver within the callback (e.g., by requiring the receiver to call a `repayFlashLoan` function). Use a check that the vault's total deposit/borrow state is not altered during the flash loan.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Event is missing `indexed` fields
findings.description: Index event fields make the field more quickly accessible to off-chain tools that parse events. However, note that each index field costs extra gas during emission, so it's not necessarily best to index the maximum allowed per event (three fields). Each event should use three indexed fields if there are three or more fields, and gas usage is not particularly of concern for the events in question. If there are fewer than three fields, all of the fields should be indexed.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L157-L165):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L158)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [borrowed[msg.sender] -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L161)
	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).

findings.message: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.description: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.recommendation: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.

findings.title: Unrestricted Oracle Price Setting
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle contract allows anyone to set the price for any token without any access control. This enables arbitrary manipulation of the token price at any time.
findings.message: An attacker can manipulate the token price to liquidate other users' positions at a discount, or to self-liquidate to avoid debt, or to inflate collateral to borrow more than allowed, effectively draining the vault of funds.
findings.recommendation: Implement access control on setPrice (e.g., only owner) and use a TWAP oracle resistant to manipulation. Consider using a trusted price feed like Chainlink.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Liquidation Does Not Update totalBorrowed and Does Not Require Debt Repayment
findings.description: The liquidate function sets the user's borrowed balance to zero without updating the global totalBorrowed variable. Additionally, it does not require the liquidator to repay the outstanding debt; it simply transfers the collateral to the liquidator. This leads to accounting inconsistency and allows an attacker to self-liquidate by manipulating oracle price, effectively stealing the borrowed amount.
findings.message: An attacker can borrow tokens, manipulate oracle to crash price, self-liquidate, and walk away with the borrowed tokens without ever repaying, causing direct loss of funds from the vault. The accounting error also prevents other users from withdrawing correctly.
findings.recommendation: Update totalBorrowed in liquidate: totalBorrowed -= borrowed[user]; Also require the liquidator to repay the debt or transfer collateral to the vault for proper debt coverage.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deposits 100 tokens as collateral. 2. Attacker borrows 60 tokens. 3. Attacker calls oracle.setPrice(token, 1) to reduce collateral value to near zero. 4. Attacker calls vault.liquidate(attacker). Condition passes, collateral[attacker]=0, borrowed[attacker]=0, and 100 tokens are transferred to attacker. totalBorrowed remains 60. Attacker now has both the borrowed 60 and the returned 100 (net gain 60). Vault is insolvent.

findings.title: Liquidation does not require debt repayment, causing vault insolvency
findings.description: The `liquidate` function transfers the entire collateral of the liquidated user to the liquidator without requiring the liquidator to repay the user's debt. The debt mapping `borrowed[user]` is set to zero, but the vault receives no tokens in return, causing a net asset loss. The missing update to `totalBorrowed` further aggravates the state inconsistency. This allows any user with a friend to artificially drain the vault: user deposits collateral, borrows, then a friend liquidates the position (even if not undercollateralized by manipulating the oracle) and receives the collateral for free, while the vault loses both the collateral and the debt repayment.
findings.message: Complete loss of protocol solvency; all user deposits and collateral can be extracted since a borrower can be liquidated at will (or by colluding with someone) and the vault's assets decrease without receiving repayment.
findings.recommendation: Redesign liquidation to require the liquidator to repay the user's debt (or seize only the minimum collateral needed). Update `totalBorrowed` by subtracting the liquidated debt. Consider using a partial liquidation mechanism.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Missing Reentrancy Protection on State-Changing Functions During Flash Loan
findings.description: The flashLoan function uses a boolean lock to prevent reentrant calls to itself, but it does not protect other state-changing functions (borrow, repay, liquidate, withdraw, deposit, addCollateral, removeCollateral). An attacker can call these functions during the callback, potentially exploiting the temporarily borrowed tokens or manipulated oracle price.
findings.message: During a flash loan, the attacker can call borrow, withdraw, or liquidate while holding borrowed tokens, enabling double-spending or accounting manipulation. Combined with oracle manipulation, this allows more sophisticated attacks.
findings.recommendation: Use a reentrancy guard modifier (e.g., OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard) on all state-changing functions, or carefully track a state variable to prevent reentrancy entirely.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: Borrow Function Relies on Manipulable Spot Price
findings.description: The borrow function calculates the maximum borrow amount based on current collateral value using getTokenPrice(), which returns a spot price from the oracle. Since the oracle price can be arbitrarily set, an attacker can temporarily inflate the price to borrow far more than their collateral is worth.
findings.message: An attacker can drain the vault by borrowing more than legitimate collateral allows, using a flash loan to manipulate the price and repay later.
findings.recommendation: Use a time-weighted average price (TWAP) oracle and require price from a trusted source. Also, implement a sanity check on price changes.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Missing update of `totalBorrowed` in `liquidate`
findings.description: When `liquidate` sets `borrowed[user] = 0`, it does not decrease `totalBorrowed`. This breaks the invariant that `totalBorrowed` equals the sum of all individual `borrowed` balances. Over time, `totalBorrowed` becomes overstated, which can cause the `borrow` function's liquidity check (`totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount`) to revert incorrectly (due to underflow in Solidity 0.8), leading to a denial of service for legitimate borrowing. Additionally, the overstated `totalBorrowed` misrepresents the vault's liabilities.
findings.message: Permanent denial of service for borrowing if many liquidations occur; incorrect accounting of protocol liabilities.
findings.recommendation: Add `totalBorrowed -= borrowed[user];` inside `liquidate` after setting `borrowed[user] = 0`.

findings.title: `removeCollateral` can bypass collateralization check via oracle manipulation
findings.description: The `removeCollateral` function uses `getTokenPrice()` to compute the new collateral value. An attacker can manipulate the oracle (e.g., through the `ManipulableOracle.setPrice` which is public) to temporarily set a high price, then call `removeCollateral` to withdraw an amount that would otherwise make the position undercollateralized. After removing the collateral, the price is restored. This effectively allows a borrower to remove collateral without being flagged as undercollateralized, increasing the protocol's risk.
findings.message: Borrowers can extract excess collateral, leaving positions undercollateralized and increasing the likelihood of bad debt.
findings.recommendation: Use a time-weighted average price (TWAP) or a trusted oracle that cannot be manipulated in a single transaction. Additionally, enforce a maximum per-block change in the oracle price or use a two-step withdrawal with a cooldown.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Flash loan callback can manipulate vault state and economic invariants

location.function: `flashLoan` function and the interplay with other functions (lines 50-80)

findings.description: During a flash loan callback, the receiver can call any of the vault's external functions (`deposit`, `withdraw`, `borrow`, `repay`, `addCollateral`, `removeCollateral`) and also manipulate the oracle price via the public `setPrice`. This allows an attacker to temporarily inflate deposits, borrow against inflated collateral, or liquidate positions at manipulated prices. The only post-flash check is the vault token balance, which can be satisfied by re-depositing borrowed funds or using liquidated collateral. This enables complex attacks that break the invariant that collateral and debt must remain consistent over a single transaction.
findings.message: Potential to extract value from the protocol, create bad debt, or cause user losses through manipulation of prices and state during a single transaction.
findings.recommendation: Implement a proper reentrancy guard that blocks all state-changing functions during a flash loan. Alternatively, isolate the flash loan function's state from other operations (e.g., use a separate contract for flash loans). Additionally, prevent oracle price changes during flash loans by using a snapshot mechanism.

findings.title: Flash loan does not enforce repayment from the receiver, relying solely on vault balance
findings.description: The `flashLoan` function expects the vault token balance after the callback to be at least `balanceBefore + fee`. However, this check can be bypassed if the attacker manipulates the vault balance during the callback via deposits/withdrawals or liquidations. For example, the attacker could deposit the borrowed amount back into the vault, artificially increasing the balance, then withdraw it after the flash loan completes (via a separate transaction). While the lock prevents reentrancy into `flashLoan`, the attacker can still use other functions to artificially inflate the balance. This undermines the core security assumption of flash loans.
findings.message: Flash loans may not be properly repaid, allowing attackers to borrow tokens without returning them, leading to loss of funds for other depositors.
findings.recommendation: Instead of a balance check, enforce repayment by transferring the principal and fee back from the receiver within the callback (e.g., by requiring the receiver to call a `repayFlashLoan` function). Use a check that the vault's total deposit/borrow state is not altered during the flash loan.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Event is missing `indexed` fields
findings.description: Index event fields make the field more quickly accessible to off-chain tools that parse events. However, note that each index field costs extra gas during emission, so it's not necessarily best to index the maximum allowed per event (three fields). Each event should use three indexed fields if there are three or more fields, and gas usage is not particularly of concern for the events in question. If there are fewer than three fields, all of the fields should be indexed.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether

findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
